questions.question_text: A Michigan landowner wants to split a 40-acre parcel without recording a plat. What is the maximum number of divisions allowed in a 10-year period?
distractors: 2 divisions
6 divisions
10 divisions
questions.explanation: The Michigan Land Division Act allows a maximum of 4 divisions from a parent parcel within any 10-year period without the requirement to plat the property.

questions.question_text: A Michigan property owner plans to divide land into parcels that will be accessed by a private road. What must be disclosed?
questions.correct_answer: The private road must be disclosed to buyers
distractors: Private roads are prohibited for divisions
Only public road access is needed
No disclosure is required for roads
questions.explanation: The Michigan Land Division Act requires disclosure of private road access. Buyers must be informed that the road is privately maintained and not a public road.

questions.question_text: A Michigan landowner divided property into 3 parcels in 2020. In 2025, she wants to create 2 more parcels from the remaining land without platting. Can she do this?
distractors: Yes, a new 10-year period started in January 2025
Yes, there is no time-based limit on land divisions
No, the land can never be lawfully divided again
questions.explanation: The Michigan Land Division Act limits divisions to 4 within a 10-year period. Having used 3 divisions in 2020, only 1 remains until the 10-year period expires in 2030.

questions.question_text: A Michigan land division must comply with which local requirements?
questions.correct_answer: Zoning and minimum parcel size standards
distractors: Only federal environmental regulations apply
Only state building codes are required
No local requirements apply to divisions
questions.explanation: Michigan land divisions must comply with local zoning ordinances and minimum parcel size requirements in addition to the state Land Division Act restrictions.

questions.question_text: A developer wants to create 8 residential lots from a single Michigan parcel in one year. What is required?
questions.correct_answer: The developer must record a subdivision plat
distractors: File two separate division applications
Obtain a variance from the 4-lot limit
Wait until the second 10-year period
questions.explanation: Since the Land Division Act limits unplatted divisions to 4 in a 10-year period, creating 8 lots requires the developer to go through the formal platting process and record a subdivision plat.

questions.question_text: A Michigan homeowner near a farm complains about manure odors. The farm follows GAAMPs. What protection does the farmer have?
questions.correct_answer: The farm is presumed not to be a nuisance
distractors: The farmer must cease all regular farm operations immediately
The farmer must pay actual damages to all affected neighbors
The homeowner can get a court injunction against the farm
questions.explanation: Under Michigan's Right to Farm Act, farms that conform to Generally Accepted Agricultural Management Practices (GAAMPs) are presumed not to be a nuisance, protecting them from neighbor complaints.

questions.question_text: A buyer is purchasing a home near farmland in Michigan. What must the seller disclose about the neighboring farm?
distractors: The farm's annual revenue
Names of all farm employees
The farm's pesticide inventory
questions.explanation: Michigan's seller disclosure requirements include mentioning proximity to farming operations. Buyers should understand that nearby farms are protected from nuisance claims under the Right to Farm Act.

questions.question_text: What does GAAMPs stand for in the context of Michigan's Right to Farm Act?
distractors: Government Approved Agriculture Methods Program
General Assessment of Agricultural Manure Policy
Graded Agricultural Acreage Measurement Plans
questions.explanation: GAAMPs stands for Generally Accepted Agricultural Management Practices. Farms that conform to GAAMPs receive nuisance protection under Michigan's Right to Farm Act.

questions.question_text: A Michigan farm does NOT follow GAAMPs and generates excessive noise. Can neighbors bring a nuisance claim?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the nuisance presumption does not apply
distractors: No, all farms are fully protected by law
Only if the farm is under 10 total acres
Only after 5 years of recorded complaints
questions.explanation: The Right to Farm Act's nuisance protection applies only to farms conforming to GAAMPs. A farm not following these practices loses the presumption and can face nuisance claims.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks an agent why the seller disclosure mentions nearby farming. What is the best explanation?
questions.correct_answer: Farms have legal protection from nuisance suits
distractors: Farm zoning increases property taxes
The seller must provide crop reports
Farms are required to share water rights
questions.explanation: Michigan requires disclosure of farming proximity because the Right to Farm Act protects conforming farms from nuisance claims. Buyers need to understand they may not be able to stop normal farming activities.

questions.question_text: MSHDA's MI Home Loan program primarily assists buyers by providing:
distractors: Free home inspections
Property tax exemptions for 5 years
Government-owned housing at no cost
questions.explanation: MSHDA (Michigan State Housing Development Authority) offers the MI Home Loan program, which provides below-market interest rate mortgages to eligible homebuyers, along with down payment assistance.

questions.question_text: Several Michigan brokers agree to charge the same commission rate in their market area. Under the Antitrust Reform Act, this is:
distractors: Acceptable standard practice
Legal if disclosed to clients
Permitted for brokers in the same firm
questions.explanation: Commission rates must be freely negotiable. Agreements among competing brokers to fix commission rates violate Michigan's Antitrust Reform Act and federal antitrust law, and can result in treble damages.

questions.question_text: In a Michigan land contract, who holds legal title to the property during the contract period?
distractors: The buyer
The title company
The county register of deeds
questions.explanation: In a Michigan land contract, the seller retains legal title while the buyer receives equitable title. Legal title transfers to the buyer only when the contract is fully paid.

questions.question_text: A Michigan land contract buyer defaults on payments. What must the seller do before taking back the property?
questions.correct_answer: Provide notice and opportunity to cure
distractors: Immediately file a quiet title action
Change the locks and reclaim the property
Record a new deed in the seller's name
questions.explanation: Michigan law requires specific forfeiture procedures for land contracts, including providing the buyer with notice of default and an opportunity to cure before the seller can reclaim the property.

questions.question_text: A buyer on a Michigan land contract has equitable title. What does this mean regarding property rights?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer has an ownership interest but not deed
distractors: The buyer can sell without the seller's consent
The buyer holds the recorded deed to property
The buyer has no insurable interest at all
questions.explanation: Equitable title gives the land contract buyer an ownership interest and the right to possess and use the property, but the actual deed (legal title) remains with the seller until the contract is fully paid.
